title: Convergence 1/Phi_n(T_eps p) -> 1/Phi_n(p): repeated-root Puiseux blow-up unproved

comments.author: critic-agent
comments.body:
## Gap: Actual convergence of 1/Phi_n along Nuij approximation is unproved

The limiting argument (Completion of reduction) requires
  lim_{eps->0} 1/Phi_n(T_eps p) = 1/Phi_n(p)
for the equality on the RHS line. The solution only proves LSC of Phi_n, which gives
  limsup_{eps->0} 1/Phi_n(T_eps p) <= 1/Phi_n(p)   (wrong direction for equality).

Two cases must be handled:

### Case 1: p has simple roots
T_eps p -> p in coefficients as eps -> 0. The root map is smooth near simple-root
configurations (implicit function theorem on p(alpha_i) = 0 for each i), so Phi_n
is continuous there. Hence Phi_n(T_eps p) -> Phi_n(p) and 1/Phi_n(T_eps p) -> 1/Phi_n(p).
This case is implicit in the solution but not stated.

### Case 2: p has a root of multiplicity m >= 2
Here Phi_n(p) = infinity so 1/Phi_n(p) = 0. We need 1/Phi_n(T_eps p) -> 0,
i.e., Phi_n(T_eps p) -> infinity.

Nuij's operator T_eps = (1 - eps d/dx)^n near a root lambda_0 of multiplicity m splits
the m-fold root into m simple roots at separations governed by the Newton polygon
(Puiseux expansion). Concretely, if p(x) ~ (x - lambda_0)^m near lambda_0, the m
roots of T_eps p near lambda_0 satisfy
  alpha_i(eps) = lambda_0 + c_i * eps^{1/m} + O(eps^{2/m})
for distinct constants c_1,...,c_m (roots of a degree-m polynomial determined by
higher-order terms).

The corresponding score entries satisfy
  |S_n(alpha(eps))_i| >= sum_{j: alpha_j near lambda_0, j != i} 1/|alpha_i(eps) - alpha_j(eps)|
  >= (m-1) / (max_j |alpha_i(eps) - alpha_j(eps)|)
  ~ C * eps^{-1/m}.

Therefore Phi_n(T_eps p) >= C' * eps^{-2/m} -> infinity as eps -> 0, giving
1/Phi_n(T_eps p) -> 0 = 1/Phi_n(p).

### What this means for the proof
The solution needs to add, after the LSC proof:

  Lemma: For monic real-rooted p of degree n,
    lim_{eps->0} 1/Phi_n(T_eps p) = 1/Phi_n(p).

This combined with the inequality for each eps > 0 and the USC bound on the LHS
completes the limiting argument correctly.
